title: Merge Sort: Divide, Conquer, Combine
subtitle: Stack bowls to trace the recursive call stack, split cards into piles, and merge them back in sorted order.
encouragement: Merge sort is the first sorting algorithm in this course that's genuinely fast: Θ(n log n) in every case. It's the same strategy used in real-world systems to sort massive datasets. The trick is divide-and-conquer: break the problem in half, trust that each half gets solved, then combine. If you did the recursion tutorial with bowls, this will feel familiar because merge sort IS recursion.
realWorldExample: Imagine you and a friend need to alphabetize 200 flash cards. Instead of sorting all 200 yourself, you split the pile in half: you each sort 100 cards, then you sit across from each other and merge your two sorted piles by comparing the card on top of each pile and placing the smaller one down first. That's merge sort.

materials.detail: Write one number on each: 5, 8, 6, 1, 9, 3, 0, 2. Big, clear numbers.
These represent the recursive call stack, just like in the recursion tutorial. Label them Bowl 1 through Bowl 4.
To label each bowl with what that recursive call is doing. You'll reuse bowls, so keep extra sticky notes handy.

setup: Lay your 8 number cards in a row: 5, 8, 6, 1, 9, 3, 0, 2.
Set up your bowl stack area to one side of the cards. The stack starts empty.
Keep sticky notes and pen within reach. You'll write a new note every time a recursive call is made.
Leave open space below the card row for merging. You'll need room to lay out two piles side by side and build a merged result.

steps.instruction: Merge sort is recursive. Each call to merge_sort does three things: split the list in half, recursively sort the left half, recursively sort the right half, then merge the two sorted halves. Each recursive call is a bowl on the stack. When a call finishes, its bowl comes off.
steps.conceptNote:
Three parts of the recursion:
1. Base case: a list of 0 or 1 elements is already sorted. Return immediately.
2. Recursive assumption: trust that merge_sort correctly sorts each half.
3. Work done by this call: split, then merge the two sorted halves back together.
steps.hint: The maximum stack depth will be 4 bowls (for 8 cards). That's log₂(8) + 1 levels.

steps.instruction: We call merge_sort([5, 8, 6, 1, 9, 3, 0, 2]). This is the first recursive call, so it goes on the stack. It has 8 elements, so it's NOT a base case. It needs to split.
steps.action: Write on a sticky note: "ms([5,8,6,1,9,3,0,2])" and below it: "SPLITTING". Attach it to Bowl 1. Place Bowl 1 on the desk.
steps.checkpoint: One bowl on the stack. Your 8 cards are still in a row.
steps.conceptNote: We'll abbreviate merge_sort as "ms" on the sticky notes so they stay legible.

steps.instruction: Bowl 1 splits the list down the middle. Slide the cards into two groups: left [5, 8, 6, 1] and right [9, 3, 0, 2]. Now Bowl 1 calls merge_sort on the left half first. It PAUSES and waits.
steps.action: Update Bowl 1's note to say "WAITING for left". Now write a new sticky note: "ms([5,8,6,1])" / "SPLITTING". Attach it to Bowl 2. Stack Bowl 2 ON TOP of Bowl 1.
steps.checkpoint: Two bowls stacked. Cards are in two groups of 4. The right group [9,3,0,2] sits untouched for now.
steps.hint: Just like the recursion tutorial: the function FREEZES at the recursive call and waits. It can't merge until both halves come back sorted.

steps.instruction: Bowl 2 splits [5, 8, 6, 1] into [5, 8] and [6, 1]. It calls merge_sort on the left: [5, 8]. Bowl 2 pauses.
steps.action: Update Bowl 2: "WAITING for left". New sticky note on Bowl 3: "ms([5,8])" / "SPLITTING". Stack Bowl 3 on top.
steps.checkpoint: Three bowls stacked. You have sub-piles: [5,8], [6,1], and [9,3,0,2].

steps.instruction: Bowl 3 splits [5, 8] into single cards: [5] and [8]. It calls merge_sort([5]). Bowl 3 pauses.
steps.action: Update Bowl 3: "WAITING for left". New note on Bowl 4: "ms([5])" / "BASE CASE!". Stack Bowl 4 on top.
steps.checkpoint: Four bowls stacked! This is the maximum depth. The 5 card is alone.
steps.conceptNote: A single card is already sorted. There's nothing to split, nothing to merge. This is the base case: just return immediately.

steps.instruction: ms([5]) returns! A single element is sorted. Remove Bowl 4 from the stack. Now Bowl 3 calls merge_sort([8]) on the right half.
steps.action: Remove Bowl 4. Write a fresh note: "ms([8])" / "BASE CASE!". Place it on a new Bowl 4 (or reuse the same bowl with a new note). Stack it on top. Then immediately remove it: [8] is also a base case.
steps.checkpoint: Bowl 4 is off again. Three bowls remain. Bowl 3 (ms([5,8])) now has both halves back: [5] and [8].
steps.conceptNote: Both recursive calls returned. Bowl 3 is no longer waiting. Now it can do its own work: merge.

steps.instruction: Bowl 3 merges! It has two sorted halves: [5] and [8]. Lay them face up. Compare the fronts and build the merged result.
steps.action: Compare 5 vs 8. 5 is smaller: place it first. 8 is all that's left: place it second. Merged result: [5, 8]. Group these two cards together.
steps.checkpoint: Bowl 3 is done. Update its note: "ms([5,8]) → [5,8] DONE". Remove Bowl 3 from the stack.

steps.instruction: Bowl 2 got its left half back sorted: [5, 8]. Now it calls merge_sort on the right half: [6, 1]. Bowl 2 updates to "WAITING for right".
steps.action: Update Bowl 2: "WAITING for right". New note on Bowl 3: "ms([6,1])" / "SPLITTING". Stack Bowl 3.
steps.checkpoint: Three bowls again. Bowl 3 is working on [6, 1].
steps.hint: Notice the bowl stack grows and shrinks like breathing. It grew to 4 for the left branch, shrank to 2, and now it's growing again for the right branch.

steps.instruction: Bowl 3 splits [6, 1] into [6] and [1]. Both are base cases (single cards), so both recursive calls return immediately.
steps.action: Briefly stack and remove Bowl 4 for ms([6]), then again for ms([1]). Both are base cases. Bowl 3 now has both halves back and is ready to merge.
steps.checkpoint: Bowl 3 has [6] and [1] returned. Time to merge.
steps.hint: You can speed up the base case bowls now that you understand the pattern. The key moment is the merge.

steps.action: Compare 6 vs 1. 1 is smaller: place it first. 6 next. Merged: [1, 6]. Group these cards. Update Bowl 3: "DONE". Remove Bowl 3.
steps.checkpoint: Two bowls remain. Bowl 2 now has BOTH halves back: [5, 8] (from left) and [1, 6] (from right).

steps.instruction: Bowl 2 merges [5, 8] and [1, 6]. This is the first merge with more than one card per pile. Use two fingers as pointers: one on the front of each pile.
steps.action:
Lay [5, 8] and [1, 6] face up, side by side. Point at the front of each:

• 5 vs 1: 1 wins. Place 1.
• 5 vs 6: 5 wins. Place 5.
• 8 vs 6: 6 wins. Place 6.
• Only 8 left. Place 8.

Merged: [1, 5, 6, 8].
steps.checkpoint: Update Bowl 2: "ms([5,8,6,1]) → [1,5,6,8] DONE". Remove Bowl 2. One bowl remains. The entire left half is sorted!
steps.conceptNote: The merge function uses two pointers. Each comparison only looks at the front of each pile. Because both piles are already sorted, the front card is always the smallest remaining element. This is why merge runs in Θ(n) time.
steps.hint: If one pile runs out before the other, just place all remaining cards from the other pile. They're already sorted.

steps.instruction: Bowl 1 got its left half back: [1, 5, 6, 8]. Now it calls merge_sort on the right half: [9, 3, 0, 2]. Same process: split, recurse, merge.
steps.action:
Update Bowl 1: "WAITING for right". Now work through the right side. You know the pattern, so let's go faster:

• Stack Bowl 2: ms([9,3,0,2]) → splits into [9,3] and [0,2]
• Stack Bowl 3: ms([9,3]) → splits into [9] and [3]
• Stack+remove Bowl 4: ms([9]) base case. Stack+remove Bowl 4: ms([3]) base case.
• Bowl 3 merges [9] and [3]: compare 9 vs 3. [3, 9]. Remove Bowl 3.
steps.checkpoint: Bowl 2 got its left half back: [3, 9]. Now it recurses right on [0, 2].

steps.action:
Stack Bowl 3: ms([0,2]) splits into [0] and [2]. Both are base cases. Bowl 3 merges: compare 0 vs 2. [0, 2]. Remove Bowl 3.

Bowl 2 now has both halves: [3, 9] and [0, 2]. Time to merge!

Lay both piles face up. Two-finger comparison:

• 3 vs 0: 0 wins. Place 0.
• 3 vs 2: 2 wins. Place 2.
• [0,2] pile is empty! Drain the rest: place 3, then 9.

Merged: [0, 2, 3, 9].
steps.checkpoint: Update Bowl 2: "DONE". Remove Bowl 2. Only Bowl 1 remains. The right half is sorted!
steps.conceptNote: When one pile empties, the "drain" step kicks in. All remaining cards are already sorted and larger than everything placed so far. Just lay them down. In the code, this is the two cleanup while loops after the main comparison loop.

steps.instruction: The final merge! Bowl 1 has both halves back: [1, 5, 6, 8] and [0, 2, 3, 9]. This is the last merge. Take your time with it.
steps.action:
Lay both sorted halves face up. Two-finger merge:

• 1 vs 0: 0 wins.
• 1 vs 2: 1 wins.
• 5 vs 2: 2 wins.
• 5 vs 3: 3 wins.
• 5 vs 9: 5 wins.
• 6 vs 9: 6 wins.
• 8 vs 9: 8 wins.
• Drain: 9.

Result: [0, 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9].
steps.checkpoint: Update Bowl 1: "ms([5,8,6,1,9,3,0,2]) → [0,1,2,3,5,6,8,9] DONE!" Remove the last bowl. The stack is empty. The list is sorted!
steps.hint: Count the comparisons: 7 to merge 8 elements. Merging two halves of total size n takes at most n−1 comparisons. That's Θ(n).

steps.instruction: Look at your collection of used sticky notes. Arrange them to see the tree shape of what you just did. The splitting goes DOWN (deeper into recursion), the merging comes back UP (as calls return).
steps.action: On your desk, arrange the sticky notes in a tree: the full-list note at the top, the two half-list notes below it, the four pair notes below those, and the eight single-card notes at the bottom. Draw arrows going down (split) and up (merge).
steps.conceptNote: The tree has log₂(n) levels. With 8 cards, that's 3 levels of splitting. At each level, the total merge work is Θ(n) because every card gets compared once. Total: Θ(n) × log₂(n) = Θ(n log n).

steps.instruction: Let's talk time complexity. You felt the stack grow to depth 4 (log₂(8) + 1) and shrink repeatedly. At every level of the tree, the total merge work touched all 8 cards. That gives us the formula.
steps.conceptNote: The recurrence is T(n) = 2 · T(n/2) + Θ(n). Two recursive calls on half the input, plus Θ(n) for splitting and merging. This solves to Θ(n log n) in ALL cases: best, worst, and average. Compare that to bubble sort's O(n²).
steps.hint: For 8 cards: bubble sort worst case uses ~28 comparisons. Your merge sort used about 17. For 1000 items the gap is enormous: ~500,000 vs ~10,000. One tradeoff: merge sort uses Θ(n) extra space for the temporary merged lists, while bubble sort is in-place.

steps.instruction: Try it yourself! Shuffle all 8 cards and run merge sort from scratch. Use the bowls to track every recursive call. Write a fresh sticky note for each call.
steps.action: Split, stack a bowl, recurse left (go deeper), hit base cases, merge back up, then recurse right. Track your place with the bowl stack: the top bowl always tells you what you're currently working on.
steps.hint: If you get lost mid-recursion, look at the top bowl. Is it WAITING for left, WAITING for right, or ready to MERGE? That tells you exactly what to do next.

steps.instruction: Bonus: Try it with just 4 cards (only 2 levels of recursion and 2 bowls max). Then try 16 cards (4 levels, 4 bowls max). Notice: doubling the cards only adds ONE more bowl to the maximum stack. That's the power of logarithmic depth.

reflection.question: Why does merge sort ALWAYS take Θ(n log n), even when the input is already sorted?
reflection.answer: Even if the input is sorted, merge sort still splits all the way down to single elements and merges back up. It doesn't check whether a sublist is already sorted. The structure of the recursion tree is always the same, so the work is always Θ(n log n). This is different from bubble sort, which can stop early in the best case.

reflection.question: During the merge step, why do we only look at the front of each pile?
reflection.answer: Because both piles are already sorted. The front card is the smallest remaining element in that pile. The smaller of the two fronts is guaranteed to be the overall smallest element not yet placed. We never need to dig deeper into either pile.

reflection.question: What did the bowl stack tell you about how recursion works in merge sort?
reflection.answer: The stack grows as we dive deeper into splits (going left-first), then shrinks as base cases return and merges complete. It grows again when we switch to the right branch. The maximum depth was 4 bowls for 8 cards: that's log₂(8) + 1. Each bowl represents a paused function waiting for its sub-calls to finish.

reflection.question: Merge sort needs extra space for the merged output. Could you merge two sorted piles without any extra space?
reflection.answer: In-place merging is possible but very complicated and typically slower in practice. Standard merge sort trades Θ(n) extra space for simplicity and speed. This is a classic space-time tradeoff.

reflection.question: If you doubled the cards from 8 to 16, how many more bowls would you need at maximum depth?
reflection.answer: Just one more! log₂(8) = 3, log₂(16) = 4. That's logarithmic growth: doubling the input only adds one level. Compare to bubble sort, where doubling the input roughly quadruples the work.
